text: Gifted education in Europe currently presents a landscape of significant contrast. While some nations have implemented sophisticated, specialized programs, others have yet to formally recognize "giftedness" within their legislative or educational frameworks. Despite these discrepancies, many countries bridge the gap through external support organizations. This article, situated within broader holistic research on the subject, examines the diverse pedagogical approaches and organizational structures used to identify and nurture high-potential youth across several European states. A persistent challenge remains the prevailing myth—even among some educators—that gifted programs are inherently elitist or that talented children will succeed without intervention. However, evidence suggests that without targeted attention and early identification, the unique potential of these students often remains unrecognized and underdeveloped. By mapping the systems in countries ranging from the United Kingdom to Slovakia, this study argues for a shift away from seeing giftedness as a guarantee of success toward viewing it as a specific educational need requiring professional expertise.
